era_context: The Peloponnesian War enters its final phase. Athens, despite recent naval victories, faces mounting pressure from Sparta and Persian-backed forces. The Thirty Tyrants have not yet been installed. In Sicily, the Carthaginian invasion continues under Hannibal. The Persian Empire remains stable under Artaxerxes II. In China, the Warring States period continues with ongoing interstate conflicts.

events.title: Battle of Arginusae
events.description: The Athenian fleet under ten generals defeated a Spartan fleet in a major naval engagement off the Arginusae Islands. This was one of Athens' last significant naval victories in the Peloponnesian War. However, the aftermath became infamous: the Athenian assembly voted to execute all ten generals for failing to rescue drowning sailors after the battle. This decision, later regretted, was a turning point in Athenian morale and political stability. The executions weakened Athenian military leadership and contributed to the city's eventual defeat.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in contemporary sources, particularly Xenophon and Plato. The battle and the subsequent trials are among the best-attested events of the Peloponnesian War. Dating to 406 BCE is secure.

events.title: Trial and execution of the Arginusae generals
events.description: Following the Battle of Arginusae, the Athenian assembly voted to execute all ten generals in a mass trial, despite legal irregularities and the absence of individual trials. This decision was driven by public anger over the failure to rescue drowning sailors. The executions included Conon (who escaped) and Diomedon. The trial became a symbol of democratic excess and mob justice. Socrates, serving as a prytanis (council member), refused to participate in the illegal proceedings, an act of conscience that Plato later immortalized in his Apology.
key_figures: Socrates (refused to participate in the trial)
events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary sources document the trial and executions. Plato and Xenophon both provide accounts. The event is dated to 406 BCE with high confidence.

events.title: Carthaginian siege of Acragas (Agrigentum)
events.description: Hannibal's Carthaginian forces besieged and captured the Greek city of Acragas (modern Agrigento), one of the wealthiest and most important Greek cities in Sicily. The siege lasted several months and resulted in the destruction of much of the city and the enslavement or death of thousands of inhabitants. The fall of Acragas was a major victory for Carthage and a severe blow to Greek power in Sicily, further destabilizing the island and contributing to the rise of Dionysius I.
events.certainty_note: Diodorus provides a detailed account of the siege. The dating to 406 BCE is secure in the historical tradition, though Diodorus wrote centuries later.

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Hellespont
events.description: Following their victory at Arginusae, Athenian forces maintained naval operations in the Hellespont, attempting to secure grain supplies from the Black Sea region and maintain control of key straits. These operations were part of the broader Athenian strategy to sustain the war effort despite mounting economic and military pressure. However, the execution of the generals after Arginusae demoralized the fleet and weakened Athenian command structure.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Athenian naval activities in this region during this period, but specific operations for 406 BCE are not always clearly dated. The general context is secure.

events.title: Artaxerxes II's court and Persian imperial administration
events.description: Artaxerxes II continued to rule the Persian Empire from his capitals at Susa and Babylon. His reign (405–358 BCE) was marked by relative stability and the consolidation of Persian power, though he faced periodic rebellions and challenges to his authority. The Persian Empire remained the dominant power in the Near East, with influence extending from Egypt to Central Asia. Persian gold and diplomacy played a crucial role in Greek affairs, including support for Sparta against Athens.
key_figures: Cyrus the Younger (satrap of Lydia, later rebel)
events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for 406 BCE in Persian history. This entry describes the broader political context. Cyrus the Younger's rebellion occurred in 401 BCE, so in 406 BCE he was still a loyal satrap.

events.title: Warring States period: Interstate conflicts in China
events.description: During the Warring States period, the various Chinese states continued their military campaigns and diplomatic maneuvering for hegemony. The state of Chu was expanding its power in central and southern China, while states like Qi, Yan, and others competed for dominance in the north and east. This era saw the development of advanced military tactics, the rise of professional armies, and the consolidation of state power.
key_figures: Various rulers of Chu, Qi, Yan, and other states (specific identities uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are often compiled centuries after events. Specific events for 406 BCE are not clearly dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the general historical context.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Battle of Arginusae is sometimes placed in 407 BCE by some sources, though 406 BCE is the more widely accepted dating. The trial of the generals is also occasionally dated to 405 BCE in some accounts. The siege of Acragas is sometimes dated to 407 BCE in alternative chronologies. These variations reflect the challenges of precise dating in ancient history, particularly for events not recorded in contemporary written sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 406 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Athenian history. Xenophon and Plato provide contemporary or near-contemporary accounts of the Battle of Arginusae and the trial of the generals. Sicilian history relies on Diodorus Siculus, who wrote centuries later but drew on earlier sources. Persian history is less well-documented for this specific year, relying on later Greek accounts and limited Persian sources. Chinese history depends on much later compilations. The bias toward Greek history is pronounced, reflecting both the survival of Greek texts and the emphasis in Western historical training data.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Arginusae led directly to the controversial trial and execution of the generals
The execution of the generals weakened Athenian military leadership and morale, contributing to the defeat at Aegospotami
The Carthaginian victories in Sicily, including the siege of Acragas, destabilized Greek power and contributed to Dionysius I's rise
